factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the market using the ANZSIC classification, repeatedly referencing “ANZSIC C2221” / “ANZSIC06 code C2221” (Structural Steel Fabricating), including in the executive summary and data methodology sections.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives employment figures like “4,100 employees (2025)” and “~5,000 workers (2021–2023),” but it does not state employment figures specifically within the 4,200–4,600 range for multiple years between 2018 and 2024. It also lacks a multi-year (2018–2024) employment series with those values.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state market revenue for the 2023–2024 period in the range of approximately NZD 1.4–1.9 billion. It mentions an IBISWorld market size of about NZD 1.8 billion for 2025–2026 and provides other estimates, but no explicit 2023–2024 revenue figure in that range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that structural steel fabrication capacity expanded from “120,000 to 150,000 tonnes” and repeats “120,000–150,000 tonnes” in multiple places, which falls within the required 120,000–150,000 tonnes per annum range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides explicit geographic locations for at least three major fabricators, including Culham Engineering (Whangarei), D&H Steel Construction (Henderson, West Auckland/Auckland), and Steel & Tube (Auckland), and also references Petone to Grenada (Lower Hutt) as a project location.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly lists HS codes within the 7207–7216 range (e.g., 7207, 7208, 7209, 7210, 7213, 7214, 7216) in its tariff table for steel products, satisfying the requirement to specify relevant HS codes for structural steel imports.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states MFN/Normal Tariff rates of 5% across HS 7207–7216 and emphasizes that many imports are 0% under FTAs, but it does not say that most structural steel inputs are duty-free under the NZ Normal Tariff nor that only some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5%. The required contrast between mostly duty-free Normal Tariff lines and a few 5% exceptions is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives City Rail Link steel quantities as ~4,000 tonnes structural steel and ~25,000 tonnes reinforcing steel, but it does not state the required ranges of 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural. The specific City Rail Link steel demand figures in the criterion are therefore not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states City Rail Link’s “Operational Opening” as the “Second half of 2026,” which is a completion/opening timeframe circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under the Te Kaha (One New Zealand Stadium) section that the project’s structural steel is “9,000 tonnes (roof construction),” which matches the criterion’s approximate 9,000-tonne roof steel figure.

The response states Te Kaha (One New Zealand Stadium) was “Opened: March 27, 2026,” not that it opens in 2026. It therefore does not contain the required statement that Te Kaha Stadium opening is in 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes a dedicated section on “New Dunedin Hospital (Project Whakatuputupu)” under major infrastructure projects and describes substantial fabricated steel elements (e.g., ~1,600 units of fabricated steel, large beams/columns), indicating it as a major project requiring significant structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly reconciles conflicting market size figures by attributing differences to scope definitions, explaining that IBISWorld’s C2221 includes reinforcing steel and other services while a narrower structural fabrication-only scope yields much smaller tonnage-based revenue estimates.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly flags that Steel & Tube and Fletcher Steel are primarily distributors/processors and that Pacific Steel is a reinforcing steel manufacturer, not a structural steel fabricator. It also labels Culham Engineering and D&H Steel Construction as structural steel fabricators, thereby distinguishing fabricators from distributors/mills/producers in the top-company identification.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that in NZ’s low/zero-tariff environment tariffs provide no meaningful buffer, so import competition and fabricator margins are driven mainly by global steel prices, NZD/USD exchange rates, and ocean freight costs rather than border protection. This is stated explicitly in Sections 3.3 and 5.1 (“Corrected causal reasoning”).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides actionable steps to obtain class-level data, including instructions to use Stats NZ Business Demography and AES via NZ.Stat/InfoShare and explicitly filter/search by ANZSIC06 code C2221 (and notes when only higher-level C222 is available). It also gives a concrete method to pull OMT CSVs and filter by HS codes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links construction-cycle timing to industry outcomes by describing a “bottom of cycle trading environment” and reduced construction activity driving revenue/volume declines, and it references a 2019 peak versus later spare-capacity increases. However, it does not explicitly connect employment or revenue trends to a specific timing relationship like “2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown.”

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists several projects with individual steel tonnages (e.g., CRL 4,000t, Te Kaha 9,000t, Christchurch Hospital 1,500t) and later gives a broad aggregated range for 2026–2031. However, it does not validate fabrication capacity/throughput by explicitly aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects into a summed total and comparing that total to stated capacity/throughput.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that Stats NZ does not publish an official structural steel fabrication tonnage time series and then proposes a triangulation method using SCNZ capacity/utilisation surveys, HS 7308 trade data, and major project steel-tonnage disclosures, including a formula combining these inputs.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly argues the opposite: it states tariffs provide “no meaningful competitive buffer” and that margin pressure is driven by global steel prices, NZD/USD exchange rates, and freight costs, not local tariff policy. It does not attribute margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: The response frequently includes explicit units (e.g., NZ$M, tonnes, %, km, MW) and sometimes specifies timeframes (FY2024, 2025, 2026). However, it does not present all quantitative data with explicit units/timeframes/precision: many figures are unitless or ambiguous (e.g., “$275 billion”, “~270+”, “Loss (reported)”, index points without consistent dates), and several ranges/approximations lack defined measurement precision.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly structured into numbered sections that map to the requested dimensions: market size and growth (Section 1), companies/top fabricators (Section 2), tariffs (Section 3), major projects (Section 4), and risks/opportunities (Section 5). These distinct headings explicitly organize the analysis by each required topic area.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response contains multiple speculative or unsupported forward-looking claims (e.g., “Outlook to 2031,” large aggregated steel-demand ranges, and broad pipeline implications) and includes promotional-leaning phrasing such as “unprecedented,” “strongly positive,” and “well-positioned.” These elements depart from a strictly professional market research tone without speculation or unsupported forward claims.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple tables with year/period entries for tonnage capacity/utilization (e.g., 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024) and also provides fiscal-year revenue history for Steel & Tube (FY2021, FY2024, FY2025, 1H FY2026). This satisfies presenting historical employment/revenue/capacity data in a structured, year-by-year format covering 2018–2024 (at least for capacity/tonnage).

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, technical register throughout, using structured headings, tables, and methodological notes. It does not use rhetorical questions or colloquial/informal phrasing (e.g., slang, conversational asides) that would be inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is extremely long and includes extensive step-by-step query instructions, repeated methodological caveats, broad pipeline context, and many tangential subsections (e.g., US tariff escalation, numerous extra projects) beyond the requested items. This adds substantial length without proportionate additional substantive value for the specific 2018–2024 market analysis deliverables.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Stats NZ “New Zealand business demography statistics” as the source for employment-related industry figures and includes a hyperlink with a publication date (e.g., “At February 2025”, released October 30, 2025). It also describes how to query Business Demography for ANZSIC06 C2221.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Stats NZ’s Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) as the primary Stats NZ source for financial/revenue data and includes direct hyperlinks to Stats NZ AES methodology and the AES 2024 provisional release. It also describes how AES would be queried for industry revenue/sales variables.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites NZ Customs tariff schedule materials, including the NZ Customs Working Tariff Document introduction and Section XV (Base Metals), and uses these as sources for HS duty rate statements.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly references Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) as a source for fabrication capacity/throughput, including citing multiple SCNZ “Structural Steel Industry Update” reports and the SCNZ industry updates page for capacity and spare-capacity-derived tonnage estimates.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references the NZ Companies Register/Companies Office as a data source, including a “Companies Office Data Extraction Methodology” section and links to the Companies Register site.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites project-specific sources for multiple major infrastructure projects, including City Rail Link (City Rail Link site/partners pages and NZ Steel CRL reinforcing steel) and Te Kaha Stadium (Wikipedia and Builders & Contractors). It also provides sourced details for a hospital project (New Dunedin Hospital via Health NZ and related announcements).
